@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/motor skills / imitation / praxis / autism / occupational therapy / speech-language therapy / developmental delays/movement imitation / motor imitation / gestural imitation / body movement copying / action imitation/9-materials-that-help-with-movement-imitation#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does imitation should be natural. babies copy facial expressions, toddlers mimic parents, preschoolers learn by watching. but my child doesn't do this with movements. they watch — i can see them watching intently — but the action doesn't transfer to their body. i demonstrate how to clap, and they just look at my hands. i show them how to wave goodbye, and they stand still. i model brushing teeth, and they hold the toothbrush but don't move it the same way i did. it's not that they can't move. they move plenty on their own terms. but copying someone else's movement? that's where the breakdown happens. we tried dance class. the teacher would do a movement, all the other kids would copy, and my child would either freeze, do something completely different, or look around confused. we tried sports where the coach demonstrates skills — same problem. even at home, when i'm trying to teach them something new by showing them, they don't pick it up the way other kids do. i have to physically guide their arms and legs through the motion. hand-over-hand for everything. simple games like 'do what i do' or 'follow the leader' are impossible. other parents play simon says with their kids — mine doesn't understand why everyone is moving. birthday party games, group activities at school, learning from peers — all of it is harder because this basic ability to copy what they see is missing. the occupational therapist mentioned 'motor imitation' and 'praxis' and something about how their brain processes visual movement information. i just know my child is struggling to learn through the most basic teaching method — demonstration. if they could imitate, so much learning would be easier. there have to be tools that help build this skill?

acceptedAnswer.text: MOTOR IMITATION DEFICIT / GESTURAL IMITATION IMPAIRMENT / DYSPRAXIA / IDEOMOTOR APRAXIA / IMITATION LEARNING DIFFICULTY! Motor imitation is the ability to observe another person's body movement and reproduce that movement with one's own body. This seemingly simple skill actually requires complex neurological processing: visual perception of the movement, body scheme awareness (knowing where one's own body parts are), motor planning (figuring out how to move one's body to match), and motor execution (actually producing the movement). Motor imitation deficits are common in autism spectrum disorder, developmental coordination disorder, apraxia of speech, and global developmental delays. The underlying mechanisms may include: impaired mirror neuron system function, poor body awareness/proprioception, motor planning difficulties (praxis), visual-motor integration problems, or attention/observation challenges. Motor imitation is foundational for learning — humans naturally learn by watching and copying. Deficits significantly impact skill acquisition, social learning, participation in group activities, and following demonstrations. Intervention focuses on building body awareness, simplifying imitation tasks, providing multi-sensory cues, and systematically developing imitation through structured practice.

mainEntity.name: What is Motor Imitation Deficit / Gestural Imitation Impairment / Dyspraxia / Ideomotor Apraxia / Imitation Learning Difficulty?

acceptedAnswer.text: Motor imitation is the ability to observe another person's body movement and reproduce that movement with one's own body. This seemingly simple skill actually requires complex neurological processing: visual perception of the movement, body scheme awareness (knowing where one's own body parts are), motor planning (figuring out how to move one's body to match), and motor execution (actually producing the movement). Motor imitation deficits are common in autism spectrum disorder, developmental coordination disorder, apraxia of speech, and global developmental delays. The underlying mechanisms may include: impaired mirror neuron system function, poor body awareness/proprioception, motor planning difficulties (praxis), visual-motor integration problems, or attention/observation challenges. Motor imitation is foundational for learning — humans naturally learn by watching and copying. Deficits significantly impact skill acquisition, social learning, participation in group activities, and following demonstrations. Intervention focuses on building body awareness, simplifying imitation tasks, providing multi-sensory cues, and systematically developing imitation through structured practice. Common signs include: Watches movement demonstrations but doesn't copy, Can perform movements spontaneously but not on imitation, Requires hand-over-hand guidance to learn new motor skills, Difficulty with gesture games (patty-cake, Itsy Bitsy Spider), Struggles with dance, exercise, or sports instruction.

mainEntity.name: How does a Full-Length Mirror (Self-Observation Tool) help with motor imitation deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: A full-length mirror provides essential visual feedback that connects what the child sees the model doing with what their own body is doing. Many children with imitation difficulties have poor body awareness — they don't have a clear internal sense of where their body parts are or what position they're in. The mirror externalizes this information. When practicing imitation in front of a mirror, the child can watch the model, attempt the movement, and see their own body simultaneously. They can compare their body position to the model and adjust. The mirror makes the invisible (body position) visible. This visual feedback loop accelerates learning by providing real-time information about movement accuracy. Price: ₹800–3,000. DIY option: Position a full-length mirror (or multiple mirrors for different angles) where imitation practice happens. Child and adult stand side by side, both visible in mirror. Adult models movement; child attempts; both watch child's reflection to see if it matches. Use verbal cues: 'Look — my arms are up. Now look at your arms. Are they up too?' The mirror provides the feedback the child's internal body sense doesn't. Practice simple movements first (arms up, hands on head), then progress to more complex sequences. The mirror is especially valuable for bilateral movements and whole-body actions. Safety: Ensure mirror is securely mounted or has stable base. Use shatterproof/safety mirror for young children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Action Cards with Photo Sequences help with motor imitation deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Action cards provide a static, repeatable visual reference for movements that can be studied at the child's own pace. Unlike live demonstration (which happens and is gone), photo cards freeze the movement in space and time. The child can look as long as needed, return to the image repeatedly, and study the body position carefully. Sequence cards show multi-step actions broken into phases, teaching the child to segment complex movements. Cards reduce the processing speed demands of live imitation — the child doesn't have to watch, remember, and execute simultaneously. They provide a visual memory support that can be referenced during practice. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Create or purchase photo-based action cards showing single positions (arms up, hands on hips, touch toes) and action sequences (clapping hands: hands apart → hands together → hands apart). Use photos of real people (ideally the child or similar children) rather than cartoon illustrations. Laminate for durability. Start with single-position cards: child looks at card, assumes the position, checks against card. Progress to sequence cards for multi-step actions. Cards become visual prompts during practice — show the card, child imitates what they see. Over time, fade to live demonstration as the skill develops. Safety: Ensure card images are clear and uncluttered. Start with simple, achievable positions before complex movements.

mainEntity.name: How does a Body Part Awareness Toys (Body Puzzle/Doll) help with motor imitation deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Before a child can imitate movement, they need a mental map of their own body — knowing what body parts they have, where they are, and what they're called. Body awareness (body schema) is foundational to motor imitation. If the child doesn't have a clear sense of 'my arms' or 'my head,' they can't intentionally move those parts on command or in imitation. Body puzzles, anatomy dolls, and body part identification games build this foundational body map. The child learns to name and locate body parts on external representations (puzzle, doll), then on themselves. Once the body map is established, imitation becomes more accessible because the child knows what 'arms up' means in terms of their own body. Price: ₹300–1,200. DIY option: Use body puzzles where the child assembles a person piece by piece, naming each part. Use dolls or stuffed animals to practice identifying body parts: 'Where's the doll's arm? Where's YOUR arm?' Play 'touch your nose, touch your tummy' games to strengthen body part identification on self. Sing body part songs (Head, Shoulders, Knees and Toes) with movements. The goal is a clear internal map of the body that precedes movement imitation. Once the child can reliably identify and point to their own body parts on command, they're ready to use those parts in imitation tasks. Safety: Ensure puzzle pieces are appropriate size for child's age. Build body awareness systematically before demanding imitation.

mainEntity.name: How does a Video Modeling Tools (Tablet/Screen) help with motor imitation deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Video modeling presents movement demonstrations in a controlled, repeatable, pauseable format. Unlike live demonstration, video can be watched as many times as needed. It can be paused at key moments so the child can freeze and study a position. It can be slowed down to show movement more clearly. For children with attention challenges, video can capture and hold attention better than live demonstration. Video modeling is evidence-based for teaching imitation skills, particularly in autism. The child watches a video of someone (peer, adult, or even themselves in 'video self-modeling') performing the target action, then imitates. Repetition of the same consistent model builds the motor memory. Price: ₹5,000–20,000 (tablet/device) or free with existing device. DIY option: Use a tablet or screen to show movement demonstrations. Options: find existing movement/action videos online, create custom videos of the target movements using family members or therapist as models, or use video self-modeling (show the child video of themselves successfully completing the movement — may need to be prompted/assisted initially). Present the video, allow repeated viewing, pause at key positions, then have child attempt imitation. Video can show the movement from the child's perspective (person facing away from camera) which may be easier than mirror-image imitation. Use slow-motion features when helpful. Safety: Limit screen time appropriately. Video is a learning tool, not a replacement for interactive practice.

mainEntity.name: How does a Ribbon Sticks / Scarves (Movement Visualization) help with motor imitation deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Ribbon sticks and movement scarves make arm and body movement visible by creating a colorful trail through the air. For children who don't naturally attend to or process body movements, the ribbon/scarf provides an attention-grabbing visual enhancement. The movement becomes more obvious and trackable. Additionally, the visual feedback is immediate — the child can see where their arm went by watching the ribbon trail. This external visualization of movement supports both attending to the model's movement and perceiving one's own movement. The flowing, colorful visual also motivates movement experimentation, making practice more engaging. Price: ₹100–400. DIY option: Use ribbon sticks (dowel with attached ribbon) or movement scarves. Adult and child each have one. Adult makes a simple movement (circle, up and down, side to side) — the ribbon shows the path. Child attempts to make the same pattern with their ribbon. The visual trail makes the movement pattern concrete and visible. Start with single-arm movements, progress to patterns and sequences. Can be used with music for added engagement. Scarves can also be used for whole-body movement (throw up, catch, wave around body). The visual enhancement makes copying easier because there's more to see. Safety: Ensure adequate space for swinging ribbons. Supervise to prevent tangling or wrapping around neck.

mainEntity.name: How does a Imitation Songs and Music (Structured Action Songs) help with motor imitation deficit?

acceptedAnswer.text: Action songs with predictable, repetitive movements provide structured imitation practice embedded in motivating musical activity. Songs like 'If You're Happy and You Know It,' 'Head, Shoulders, Knees and Toes,' and 'The Wheels on the Bus' pair specific actions with specific lyrics, providing auditory cues for when to move and what to do. The music motivates engagement, the repetition allows multiple practice attempts, and the predictability builds anticipation. Over time, the child learns the song and begins to anticipate the movements, transitioning from reactive imitation to predictive movement. Music also supports motor rhythm and timing, which are components of skilled movement. Price: Free – ₹500 (music player or song resources). DIY option: Choose action songs appropriate for child's current level. Start with songs with simple, single actions (clap your hands, stomp your feet). Progress to songs with changing actions (If You're Happy and You Know It with verses), then to songs with sequences (Hokey Pokey, Macarena for older children). Perform together, demonstrating the movements. Accept approximations initially and shape toward accuracy. The song structure cues when movements happen; repetition provides practice. Can use video versions if helpful for consistent demonstration. Daily song time builds imitation practice into routine in an engaging way. Safety: Choose songs with developmentally appropriate movements. Adapt as needed for child's physical abilities.

mainEntity.name: What are the warning signs that need professional help?

acceptedAnswer.text: REAL WORLD EVIDENCE from Pinnacle Blooms Network: 20M+ Exclusive 1:1 Sessions. 97%+ Measured Improvement. 70+ Centers.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/motor skills / imitation / praxis / autism / occupational therapy / speech-language therapy / developmental delays/movement imitation / motor imitation / gestural imitation / body movement copying / action imitation/9-materials-that-help-with-movement-imitation#howto
@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Motor Imitation Deficit
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Motor imitation requires the integration of multiple systems: visual attention to the model, visual processing of the movement, body schema (knowing one's own body), motor planning (translating seen movement into motor commands), and motor execution (producing the movement). Deficits can occur at any of these stages, and intervention must be tailored to the specific breakdown point. For many children, building foundational body awareness and providing enhanced visual feedback (mirrors, video) bridges the gap. For others, proprioceptive enhancement is key. Systematic practice with appropriate supports builds the neural pathways for imitation that aren't developing automatically.

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: Motor imitation is the ability to observe another person's body movement and reproduce that movement with one's own body. This seemingly simple skill actually requires complex neurological processing: visual perception of the movement, body scheme awareness (knowing where one's own body parts are), motor planning (figuring out how to move one's body to match), and motor execution (actually producing the movement). Motor imitation deficits are common in autism spectrum disorder, developmental coordination disorder, apraxia of speech, and global developmental delays. The underlying mechanisms may include: impaired mirror neuron system function, poor body awareness/proprioception, motor planning difficulties (praxis), visual-motor integration problems, or attention/observation challenges. Motor imitation is foundational for learning — humans naturally learn by watching and copying. Deficits significantly impact skill acquisition, social learning, participation in group activities, and following demonstrations. Intervention focuses on building body awareness, simplifying imitation tasks, providing multi-sensory cues, and systematically developing imitation through structured practice. Motor imitation requires the integration of multiple systems: visual attention to the model, visual processing of the movement, body schema (knowing one's own body), motor planning (translating seen movement into motor commands), and motor execution (producing the movement). Deficits can occur at any of these stages, and intervention must be tailored to the specific breakdown point. For many children, building foundational body awareness and providing enhanced visual feedback (mirrors, video) bridges the gap. For others, proprioceptive enhancement is key. Systematic practice with appropriate supports builds the neural pathways for imitation that aren't developing automatically.

step.name: Start with Foundation Materials
step.text: Begin with: Full-Length Mirror (Self-Observation Tool), Action Cards with Photo Sequences, Body Part Awareness Toys (Body Puzzle/Doll).

signOrSymptom.name: Watches movement demonstrations but doesn't copy
Can perform movements spontaneously but not on imitation
Requires hand-over-hand guidance to learn new motor skills

Struggles with dance, exercise, or sports instruction

@graph.name: 9 Materials for Motor Imitation Deficit
@graph.description: Evidence-based materials for ages 18 months – 10 years

item.name: Full-Length Mirror (Self-Observation Tool)
item.description: A full-length mirror provides essential visual feedback that connects what the child sees the model doing with what their own body is doing. Many children with imitation difficulties have poor body awareness — they don't have a clear internal sense of where their body parts are or what position they're in. The mirror externalizes this information. When practicing imitation in front of a mirror, the child can watch the model, attempt the movement, and see their own body simultaneously. They can compare their body position to the model and adjust. The mirror makes the invisible (body position) visible. This visual feedback loop accelerates learning by providing real-time information about movement accuracy.

Adult models movement; child attempts; both watch child's reflection to see if it matches. Use verbal cues: 'Look — my arms are up. Now look at your arms. Are they up too?' The mirror provides the feedback the child's internal body sense doesn't. Practice simple movements first (arms up, hands on head), then progress to more complex sequences. The mirror is especially valuable for bilateral movements and whole-body actions.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure mirror is securely mounted or has stable base. Use shatterproof/safety mirror for young children.

item.name: Action Cards with Photo Sequences
item.description: Action cards provide a static, repeatable visual reference for movements that can be studied at the child's own pace. Unlike live demonstration (which happens and is gone), photo cards freeze the movement in space and time. The child can look as long as needed, return to the image repeatedly, and study the body position carefully. Sequence cards show multi-step actions broken into phases, teaching the child to segment complex movements. Cards reduce the processing speed demands of live imitation — the child doesn't have to watch, remember, and execute simultaneously. They provide a visual memory support that can be referenced during practice.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option

Use photos of real people (ideally the child or similar children) rather than cartoon illustrations. Laminate for durability. Start with single-position cards: child looks at card, assumes the position, checks against card. Progress to sequence cards for multi-step actions. Cards become visual prompts during practice — show the card, child imitates what they see. Over time, fade to live demonstration as the skill develops.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note

item.name: Body Part Awareness Toys (Body Puzzle/Doll)
item.description: Before a child can imitate movement, they need a mental map of their own body — knowing what body parts they have, where they are, and what they're called. Body awareness (body schema) is foundational to motor imitation. If the child doesn't have a clear sense of 'my arms' or 'my head,' they can't intentionally move those parts on command or in imitation. Body puzzles, anatomy dolls, and body part identification games build this foundational body map. The child learns to name and locate body parts on external representations (puzzle, doll), then on themselves. Once the body map is established, imitation becomes more accessible because the child knows what 'arms up' means in terms of their own body.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use body puzzles where the child assembles a person piece by piece, naming each part. Use dolls or stuffed animals to practice identifying body parts: 'Where's the doll's arm? Where's YOUR arm?' Play 'touch your nose, touch your tummy' games to strengthen body part identification on self. Sing body part songs (Head, Shoulders, Knees and Toes) with movements. The goal is a clear internal map of the body that precedes movement imitation. Once the child can reliably identify and point to their own body parts on command, they're ready to use those parts in imitation tasks.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure puzzle pieces are appropriate size for child's age. Build body awareness systematically before demanding imitation.

item.name: Video Modeling Tools (Tablet/Screen)
item.description: Video modeling presents movement demonstrations in a controlled, repeatable, pauseable format. Unlike live demonstration, video can be watched as many times as needed. It can be paused at key moments so the child can freeze and study a position. It can be slowed down to show movement more clearly. For children with attention challenges, video can capture and hold attention better than live demonstration. Video modeling is evidence-based for teaching imitation skills, particularly in autism. The child watches a video of someone (peer, adult, or even themselves in 'video self-modeling') performing the target action, then imitates. Repetition of the same consistent model builds the motor memory.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use a tablet or screen to show movement demonstrations. Options: find existing movement/action videos online, create custom videos of the target movements using family members or therapist as models, or use video self-modeling (show the child video of themselves successfully completing the movement — may need to be prompted/assisted initially). Present the video, allow repeated viewing, pause at key positions, then have child attempt imitation. Video can show the movement from the child's perspective (person facing away from camera) which may be easier than mirror-image imitation. Use slow-motion features when helpful.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Limit screen time appropriately. Video is a learning tool, not a replacement for interactive practice.

item.name: Ribbon Sticks / Scarves (Movement Visualization)
item.description: Ribbon sticks and movement scarves make arm and body movement visible by creating a colorful trail through the air. For children who don't naturally attend to or process body movements, the ribbon/scarf provides an attention-grabbing visual enhancement. The movement becomes more obvious and trackable. Additionally, the visual feedback is immediate — the child can see where their arm went by watching the ribbon trail. This external visualization of movement supports both attending to the model's movement and perceiving one's own movement. The flowing, colorful visual also motivates movement experimentation, making practice more engaging.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use ribbon sticks (dowel with attached ribbon) or movement scarves. Adult and child each have one. Adult makes a simple movement (circle, up and down, side to side) — the ribbon shows the path. Child attempts to make the same pattern with their ribbon. The visual trail makes the movement pattern concrete and visible. Start with single-arm movements, progress to patterns and sequences. Can be used with music for added engagement. Scarves can also be used for whole-body movement (throw up, catch, wave around body). The visual enhancement makes copying easier because there's more to see.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure adequate space for swinging ribbons. Supervise to prevent tangling or wrapping around neck.

item.name: Imitation Songs and Music (Structured Action Songs)
item.description: Action songs with predictable, repetitive movements provide structured imitation practice embedded in motivating musical activity. Songs like 'If You're Happy and You Know It,' 'Head, Shoulders, Knees and Toes,' and 'The Wheels on the Bus' pair specific actions with specific lyrics, providing auditory cues for when to move and what to do. The music motivates engagement, the repetition allows multiple practice attempts, and the predictability builds anticipation. Over time, the child learns the song and begins to anticipate the movements, transitioning from reactive imitation to predictive movement. Music also supports motor rhythm and timing, which are components of skilled movement.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Choose action songs appropriate for child's current level. Start with songs with simple, single actions (clap your hands, stomp your feet). Progress to songs with changing actions (If You're Happy and You Know It with verses), then to songs with sequences (Hokey Pokey, Macarena for older children). Perform together, demonstrating the movements. Accept approximations initially and shape toward accuracy. The song structure cues when movements happen; repetition provides practice. Can use video versions if helpful for consistent demonstration. Daily song time builds imitation practice into routine in an engaging way.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Choose songs with developmentally appropriate movements. Adapt as needed for child's physical abilities.

item.name: Weighted Lap Pad / Compression Vest (Proprioceptive Input)
item.description: Proprioception — the sense of body position and movement — is foundational to motor imitation. If the child doesn't receive clear proprioceptive signals from their muscles and joints, they don't know where their body is in space, making it nearly impossible to adjust their body to match a model. Weighted lap pads and compression vests provide enhanced proprioceptive input, increasing the child's awareness of their body. This heightened body awareness supports imitation by giving the child better information about their starting position and their movement. For children with poor proprioceptive processing, weighted and compression tools can 'wake up' the body sense that imitation depends on.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use OT-recommended weighted lap pads or compression vests before and during imitation practice. The proprioceptive input helps 'ground' the child in their body. Combine with other body awareness activities: joint compressions, bear hugs, wall push-ups, jumping. The goal is to increase the sensory information available from the body so the child has a clearer sense of their starting position and their movements. Some children benefit from wearing compression during imitation practice; others benefit from a proprioceptive warm-up before practice. Work with an OT to determine appropriate weight and compression levels.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Weighted and compression tools should be used with occupational therapist guidance. Appropriate weight depends on child's size and needs.

item.name: Gross Motor Imitation Game Cards
item.description: Gross motor imitation game cards turn imitation practice into a structured game with clear targets. Each card shows or describes a body position or action to imitate. The game format makes practice motivating, the cards provide specific targets, and the structure supports graduated difficulty progression. Cards can be organized from simple (single body part, static position) to complex (multi-step sequences, dynamic movements). The game format also supports turn-taking — child and adult can take turns drawing cards and modeling/imitating. This shifts imitation practice from drill-like repetition to playful interaction, increasing engagement and motivation.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create or purchase gross motor imitation cards. Organize by difficulty: Level 1 — single static positions (arms up, hands on head, touch toes), Level 2 — single dynamic actions (jump, spin, wave), Level 3 — two-step sequences (clap then stomp, arms up then touch toes), Level 4 — multi-step or complex movements. To play: draw a card, adult demonstrates (or child interprets from picture), child imitates. Can use reward systems for successful imitation. Can also play where child draws card and demonstrates for adult to imitate — practicing on both sides of the imitation exchange. Keep sessions short and success-oriented.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure adequate space for movement activities shown on cards. Match card difficulty to child's current ability level.

item.name: Laser Pointer / Flashlight for Body Part Targeting
item.description: A laser pointer or flashlight provides a visual cue that directs attention to specific body parts during imitation instruction. For children who have difficulty knowing which body part to move or where to direct their attention, the light spot provides an unmissable visual target. The adult can shine the light on their own arm while saying 'move your arm,' then shine it on the child's arm to prompt them to move that same part. This external visual cue supports the internal body mapping that imitation requires. The light is especially useful for teaching movements involving specific body parts that the child may not reliably identify.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use a flashlight or safe laser pointer during imitation teaching. Shine on your body part as you demonstrate: 'Watch my arm' (light on your arm) — then move it. Shine on child's corresponding body part: 'Now your arm' (light on their arm). The light directs visual attention and highlights the relevant body part. This is especially helpful when teaching movements involving less-attended body parts (elbows, shoulders, knees). Can also use for sequencing: light on first body part to move, then second. Combine with verbal cues and mirror for maximum support. The light fades as the child develops reliable body part identification.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: NEVER shine laser or bright light toward eyes. Use only on body/limbs. Supervise use carefully.

socialMedia.caption: When copying actions doesn't come naturally. Some children watch but can't imitate movements — making learning through demonstration nearly impossible. Here are 9 materials that help build movement imitation: full-length mirrors, action cards, body awareness toys, video modeling, ribbon sticks, action songs, proprioceptive tools, imitation game cards, and visual targeting tools. Build the skill that unlocks learning through watching. 🔖 #MovementImitation #MotorSkills #PinnacleBlooms
